questions.q: Saga pattern vs 2-phase commit. Saga's key advantage?
choices: No blocking locks across services — each service commits locally; compensate on failure
questions.explanation: 2PC holds locks across all participants until the coordinator decides. Fragile + slow across services. Saga: each step commits independently; failures trigger compensating actions. Eventual consistency tradeoff.

choices: Append-only log of events; derive state by replay
questions.explanation: Events are facts; state is derived. Replay to any point in time. Great audit trail. Downside: replay time grows → snapshots every N events. Popular for finance, ordering, gaming systems.

questions.q: CQRS = Command-Query Responsibility Segregation. What does it separate?
choices: Write model (commands) and read model (queries) — often different stores
questions.explanation: Writes go to one model (OLTP, normalized). Reads go to a different model (denormalized, search index, materialized views). Updated via events. Scales reads + writes independently at cost of eventual consistency.

questions.q: You have a monolith. Revenue is growing. When is the RIGHT time to split into microservices?
choices: When team coordination + deploy friction exceed the cost of microservice infrastructure
questions.explanation: Microservices solve org-scaling problems (autonomous teams, independent deploys). Premature split = complexity without benefit. Extract when a bounded context has a clear, independent team.

choices: Isolate domain logic from external concerns (DB, HTTP) so it's swappable/testable
questions.explanation: Domain at the center; adapters for HTTP/DB/queue/etc wire in. Lets you test domain without I/O. Swap a Postgres adapter for in-memory in tests. The testability gain > the overhead.

questions.q: Multi-tenancy: which provides the STRONGEST isolation at highest cost?
questions.explanation: Full cluster per tenant = blast radius = one tenant; max resource isolation; highest ops cost. Shared schema = cheapest, weakest isolation. Most SaaS trade off in the middle (schema or DB per tenant).

questions.q: BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern. Who consumes it?
choices: A specific client type — one BFF per (web, iOS, Android) shape
questions.explanation: Each client has different needs (mobile: minimal payloads; web: richer). BFF layer tailors the API surface per client. Wraps N microservices into one client-optimized API.
